System_Architecture: The system architecture includes a single-agent (SARL) and multi-agent (MARL) environment. SARL uses a controller to manage nodes, while MARL has each node as an agent. The RL model employs DDQN with PER, and the state and reward are based on estimated E2E latencies.
Agent_Role: In SARL, the controller acts as the agent, receiving state information from nodes and sending control messages. In MARL, each node is an agent, making independent decisions based on local queue information.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism uses DDQN with PER for learning. The agent selects queues to service based on estimated E2E latencies, aiming to minimize the maximum E2E latency. State includes queue information, and reward is based on transmitted and remaining packets' estimated delays.
Evaluation_Method: Simulations on four network topologies comparing the RL-based scheduler with FIFO, RR, NAT-HA, and MED-HA. Metrics include maximum E2E latency, latency fairness index, and maximum latency fairness index.
Experimental_Design: Experiments use fixed and random packet generation scenarios. The RL model is implemented with TensorFlow, using hyperparameters like learning rate, discount factor, and batch size. Topologies include linear, tree, single-node, and cyclic structures.
Challenge_Type: Ensuring fair distribution of E2E latency in networks, especially for services like metaverse, and addressing large state and action spaces in RL for network scheduling.
Research_Gap: Existing studies focus on deadline achievement rather than E2E latency fairness. There is a gap in using RL to minimize maximum E2E latency and improve fairness in dynamic network environments.
Evaluation_Limitations: Simulations assume fixed transmission delay and infinite queue capacity, which may not fully reflect real-world network conditions. The RL model's performance in extremely large-scale networks is untested.
Authors: Juhyeok Kwon, Jihye Ryu, Jee Hang Lee, Jinoo Joung

Title: Joint Quantum Reinforcement Learning and Stabilized Control for Spatio-Temporal Coordination in Metaverse
Abstract: This paper proposes a novel system-wide coordination algorithm for high synchronization between physical-space and virtual meta-space in metaverse systems. The algorithm consists of three stages: QMARL-based scheduling for low-delay temporal synchronization, avatar-popularity-aware virtual content creation for spatial synchronization, and AoI-aware caching strategies for faster spatio-temporal synchronization.
Keywords: Age-of-Information, metaverse, quantum reinforcement learning, synchronization
System_Architecture: Three-stage architecture (QMARL-based scheduler, synchronization-centric meta-space control, dynamic caching strategy)
Agent_Role: Service Providers (SPs), Observers, Metaverse Server
Behavior_Mechanism: QMARL-based scheduling, Lyapunov optimization, Avatar-popularity-aware control
Evaluation_Method: Simulation-based evaluation with system parameters and benchmarks
Experimental_Design: Physical-space and meta-space maps (2,500 m × 2,500 m), Observer velocity (20 m/s), Data quality levels (4 levels)
Challenge_Type: High-dimensional action spaces, Scalability in NISQ era, Stability in storage occupancy
Research_Gap: First to differentiate AoI into physical and virtual AoI, First to utilize QMARL for metaverse data gathering
Evaluation_Limitations: Limited to simulation, No real-world deployment
Authors: Soohyun Park, Jaehyun Chung, Changyoung Park, Soyi Jung, Minscok Choi, Sungrae Cho, Joongheon Kim

Title: MARL Sim2real Transfer: Merging Physical Reality With Digital Virtuality in Metaverse
Abstract: Metaverse is an artificial virtual world mapped from and interacting with the real world. In metaverse, digital entities coexist with their physical counterparts. Powered by deep learning, metaverse is inevitably becoming more intelligent in the interactions between reality and virtuality. However, it is confronted with a nontrivial problem known as sim2real transfer when deep learning techniques try to bridge the reality gap between the physical world and simulations. In this article, we use multiagent deep reinforcement learning (MARL) to implement collective intelligence for digital entities as well as their physical counterparts. To model the immersive environments in metaverse, we define a nonstationary variant of Markov games and propose a recurrent MARL solution to it. Based on the solution, MARL sim2real transfer that bridges real and virtual multiple unmanned aerial vehicle (multi-UAV) systems is successfully conducted by employing recurrent multiagent deep deterministic policy gradient (R-MADDPG) with the domain randomization technique. Additionally, we use perception-control modularization to improve the generalization performance of MARL policies and make training more efficient.
Keywords: Metaverse, multiagent deep reinforcement learning (MARL), multiple unmanned aerial vehicle (multi-UAV), nonstationary Markov game, sim2real transfer
Research_Area: The system architecture integrates metaverse with multi-UAV systems, featuring a nonstationary Markov game model for dynamic environments. It includes simulation (Airsim) and physical UAV systems, with perception-control modularization for target detection and MARL-based control.
System_Architecture: UAVs act as agents in both simulated and physical environments, collaborating to deliver goods. Each UAV functions as an autonomous agent with private observations and shared rewards in a cooperative task.
Agent_Role: The behavior mechanism uses nonstationary Markov games to model dynamic environments, solved by recurrent MARL (R-MADDPG) with domain randomization. Perception-control modularization divides tasks into CNN-based target detection and MARL-based control, enhancing generalization.
Behavior_Mechanism: Simulation-based evaluation on Airsim with domain randomization, comparing R-MADDPG against baseline policies. Real-world experiments on physical multi-UAV systems measuring completion rates and timings.
Evaluation_Method: Experiments involve 2-UAV cooperative delivery tasks in simulated (Airsim) and real-world environments. Domain parameters (noise, flight height, distances) are randomized. Training uses 20,000 episodes with ADAM optimizer.
Experimental_Design: Bridging the reality gap between simulations and physical UAV systems, handling nonstationary environments, and improving policy generalization.
Challenge_Type: Most MARL studies lack real-world validation. Existing sim2real transfer methods ignore nonstationary Markov games and memory-based policies, limiting adaptability to dynamic systems.
Research_Gap: Most multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) studies are confined to simulation environments, lacking real-world validation. Existing sim2real transfer methods fail to adequately address nonstationary Markov game models and memory-based policy design, thus limiting their adaptability to dynamic and uncertain real-world systems.
Evaluation_Limitations: Simulations may not fully replicate real-world dynamics (e.g., wind, sensor errors). Physical experiments are limited to small-scale UAV teams, requiring larger-scale validation.
Authors: Haoran Shi, Guanjun Liu, Kaiwen Zhang, Ziyuan Zhou, Jiacun Wang
Source_Name: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems

Title: MADRL-Based Joint Edge Caching and Bitrate Selection for Multicategory 360° Video Streaming
Abstract: This article proposes a multiagent deep-reinforcement-learning-based joint edge caching and bitrate selection strategy for multicategory 360° video streaming to improve users’ average Quality of Experience (QoE). The strategy employs different edge caching and bitrate selection strategies for different video categories to enable fine-grained performance optimization.
Keywords: Bitrate selection, edge caching, multiagent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL), multicategory 360° video streaming, Quality of Experience (QoE)
Research_Area: Edge Caching, Bitrate Selection, Multiagent Deep Reinforcement Learning
System_Architecture: Multiagent cooperative Markov decision process (MDP) with FoV-aware operation
Agent_Role: Video Categories (Agents)
Behavior_Mechanism: FoV-aware Multiagent Soft Actor-Critic (FA-MASAC) algorithm
Evaluation_Method: Simulation-based evaluation with real-world datasets
Experimental_Design: Trace-driven experiments with two datasets (Data Set1 and Data Set2), varying cache sizes (5%-25%), and user request proportions (10%-50%)
Challenge_Type: High-dimensional decision space, Dynamic user requests, Diverse QoE requirements
Research_Gap: Neglect of diverse QoE requirements in existing QoE-driven strategies
Evaluation_Limitations: Limited to simulation, No real-world deployment, Fixed video duration assumption
Authors: Jiaxin Zeng, Xiaobo Zhou, Keqiu Li

Title: Metaversal intelligence: Unifying human-AI interactions in human-in-the-loop AIB-Metaverse
Abstract: The convergence of 6G and Web 3 technologies with the Metaverse necessitates user-centric, intelligent solutions to address challenges of adaptability, scalability, and efficiency in decentralized systems. This paper introduces the Human-AI Blockchain (HAIB) Metaverse architecture and the Intelligent Smart Contract (InSC) framework, which integrate Reinforcement Learning (RL) and extended stigmergy to optimize multi-agent interactions and enable dynamic, self-executing smart contracts. The HAIB architecture enhances human-AI collaboration through a human-in-the-loop approach, facilitating adaptive decision-making and efficient resource allocation. Serving as the cognitive core, the InSC framework combines dynamic environmental feedback with real-time data analytics to enable intelligent decision-making. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the proposed framework reduces gas consumption by 32–33% while increasing cumulative rewards. These findings highlight the significant potential of intelligent smart contracts in advancing decentralized intelligence within the Metaverse.
Keywords: Blockchain, Extended stigmergy, HAIB-Metaverse architecture, Intelligent smart contract, Reinforcement learning algorithms, Web 3
Research_Area: Development of human-AI interaction systems in the Metaverse through intelligent smart contracts and extended stigmergy
System_Architecture: The system architecture includes the HAIB-Metaverse with layers: Interaction Layer, Human-in-the-Loop/AI Interactive Layer, and Smart Decision Layer. It integrates RL, extended stigmergy, and on/off-chain methodology.
Agent_Role: Agents include RL agents, extended stigmergic agents, and human users. RL agents optimize actions using DQL, extended stigmergic agents facilitate indirect communication, and humans provide input through the interactive layer.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism uses DQL for adaptive decision-making, extended stigmergy for dynamic environmental interaction, and on/off-chain separation for efficient execution. Stigmergy updates are based on transaction success rate and Q-values.
Evaluation_Method: Simulations comparing conventional vs. extended stigmergy on gas usage and cumulative rewards. Evaluation of InSC framework against existing gas optimization methods.
Experimental_Design: Hybrid on-chain (Solidity) and off-chain (Python) simulation environment on Sepolia Testnet. Parameters include learning rate, discount factor, and number of agents. Experiments conducted over 100 episodes.
Challenge_Type: Adaptability, scalability, and efficiency in decentralized Metaverse systems. Bridging human-AI interaction gaps in dynamic environments.
Research_Gap: Existing smart contracts lack adaptability and self-awareness. There is a gap in integrating human intelligence with AI for collective decision-making in the Metaverse.
Evaluation_Limitations: Simulations focus on specific parameters and may not fully represent complex real-world Metaverse scenarios. The framework’s performance in large-scale, highly dynamic environments needs further validation.
Authors: Minoo Soltanshahi, Martin Maier

Title: Multi-Agent DRL for Queue-Aware Task Offloading in Hierarchical MEC-Enabled Air-Ground Networks
Abstract: Mobile edge computing (MEC)-enabled air-ground networks advance 6G wireless networks by utilizing aerial base stations (ABSs) such as unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and high altitude platform stations (HAPS) to provide dynamic services to ground IoT devices (IoTDs). These IoTDs support real-time applications like multimedia and Metaverse services, which demand high computational resources and strict quality of service (QoS) guarantees, specifically in terms of latency and efficient task queue management. However, IoTDs often face constraints in energy and computational power, requiring efficient queue management and task scheduling to maintain QoS. To address these challenges, UAVs and HAPS are deployed to supplement the computational limitations of IoTDs by offloading tasks for distributed processing. Due to UAVs' resource limitations, particularly in terms of power and coverage area, HAPS are used to enhance their capabilities and extend coverage. Overloaded UAVs may relay tasks to HAPS, creating a multi-tier computing system. This paper addresses the overall energy minimization problem in the MEC-enabled air-ground integrated network (MAGIN) by optimizing UAV trajectories, computing resource allocation, and queue-aware task offloading decisions. The optimization problem is highly complex due to the nonconvex and nonlinear nature of this hierarchical system, which traditional methods cannot effectively solve. To tackle this, we reformulate the problem as a multi-agent Markov decision process (MDP) with continuous action spaces and heterogeneous agents. We propose a novel variant of multi-agent proximal policy optimization (MAPPO) with Beta distribution (MAPPO-BD) to solve this problem. Extensive simulations show that MAPPO-BD significantly outperforms other baselines, achieving superior energy savings and efficient resource management in MAGIN, while adhering to constraints related to queue delays and edge computing capabilities.
Keywords: Mobile edge computing, multi-agent deep reinforcement learning, edge network, air-ground network, Metaverse
System_Architecture: Hierarchical multi-tier computing (IoTDs, UAVs, HAPS)
Agent_Role: IoTD agents: optimize offloading ratio; UAV agents: optimize trajectories and resource allocation; HAPS agent: optimize resource allocation
Behavior_Mechanism: MAPPO-BD (Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization with Beta Distribution)
Evaluation_Method: Simulation-based comparison with MADDPG, MAPPO-ND, and PO-MAPPO-BD
Experimental_Design: Coverage area: 1x1 Km; IoTDs: 50-100; UAVs: 2-5; Hotspots: 4-7; Task size: 0.15-0.45 MB; Delay: 100-500 ms
Challenge_Type: Energy minimization, latency constraints, resource allocation
Research_Gap: Existing hierarchical frameworks often neglect dynamic queue management and efficient UAV-HAPS coordination
Evaluation_Limitations: Limited to simulation; lacks real-world dynamic conditions (e.g., varying interference, unpredictable mobility)
Authors: Muhammet Hevesli, Abegaz Mohammed Seid, Aiman Erbad, Mohamed Abdallah
Source_Name: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking

Title: Metaverse Community Group Loyalty Contagion and Regulation Model Based on User Stickiness
Abstract: The investigation into the micro-social behaviours of groups in metaverse communities is minimal. This paper proposes a user stickiness-based loyalty contagion and regulation model in a metaverse community inspired by user stickiness in the real world. We hope to establish a link between user stickiness and group loyalty through community regulation, enhance user stickiness, and improve metaverse community loyalty. We verified the feasibility and robustness of the model through simulation experiments and obtained the optimal community regulation intensity through sensitivity analysis experiments. Our results show that communities can maintain healthy long-term development when optimal community regulation intensity is adopted.
Keywords: Metaverse ecosystem, Social behaviour modelling, User stickiness, Loyalty, Group simulation, Multi-Agents
Research_Area: Micro-social behavior modeling and loyalty regulation in metaverse communities based on user stickiness
System_Architecture: The system architecture includes the AIR (Agent Index Regulation) model with four modules: environment, agent, index, and regulation. The model simulates user behavior and community loyalty changes.
Agent_Role: Agents represent users in the metaverse community, exhibiting behavior preferences and area changes based on user stickiness and environmental influences.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism uses a multi-agent system to model user behavior changes, with community regulation maintaining loyalty index through feedback from the index module.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments to verify model feasibility and animation demonstration; sensitivity analysis experiments to determine optimal community regulation intensity.
Experimental_Design: Simulation experiments with different agent numbers and community regulation intensities; sensitivity analysis using community loyalty index data at specific time nodes.
Challenge_Type: Maintaining healthy long-term development of metaverse communities and establishing the link between user stickiness and community loyalty.
Research_Gap: Limited research on micro-social behaviors of groups in metaverse communities and the impact of user stickiness on community loyalty.
Evaluation_Limitations: The model needs migration to real-world metaverse communities and consideration of unique VR properties like perception, immersion, and embodiment.
Authors: Junxiao Xue, Mingchuang Zhang
Source_Name: 2023 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces Abstracts and Workshops (VRW)

Title: Metaverse: A Solution to the Multi-Agent Value Alignment Problem
Abstract: AI Safety researchers attempting to align values of highly capable intelligent systems with those of humanity face a number of challenges including personal value extraction, multi-agent value merger and finally in-silico encoding. In this paper we assume that the value extraction problem will be solved and propose a possible way to implement an AI solution which optimally aligns with individual preferences of each user via Individual Simulated Universes (ISUs). We conclude by analyzing benefits and limitations of the proposed approach.
Keywords: AI Safety; Alternate Reality; Simulation; Value Alignment Problem; VR
System_Architecture: Individual Simulated Universes (ISUs) running on highly intelligent AI platforms
Agent_Role: Superintelligent AI agents act as environment controllers aligned with personal user values
Behavior_Mechanism: Personalized simulation aligned to Personal Coherent Extrapolated Volition (CEV)
Evaluation_Method: Conceptual analysis; theoretical evaluation
Experimental_Design: No empirical experiment; relies on philosophical and technical argumentation
Challenge_Type: Multi-agent value alignment; value aggregation
Research_Gap: Value merger deemed unsolvable; proposed ISUs as workaround
Evaluation_Limitations: No empirical testing; philosophical and technical implications discussed but not validated
Authors: Roman Yampolskiy
Source_Name: Journal of Artificial Intelligence and Consciousness

Title: Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Digital Twin over 6G Wireless Communication in the Metaverse
Abstract: Technology advancements in wireless communications and high-performance Extended Reality (XR) have empowered the developments of the Metaverse. The demand for Metaverse applications and hence, real-time digital twinning of real-world scenes is increasing. Nevertheless, the replication of 2D physical world images into 3D virtual world scenes is computationally intensive and requires computation offloading. The disparity in transmitted scene dimension (2D as opposed to 3D) leads to asymmetric data sizes in uplink (UL) and downlink (DL). To ensure the reliability and low latency of the system, we consider an asynchronous joint UL-DL scenario where the smaller data size of the physical world scenes captured in the UL will be uploaded to the Metaverse Console (MC) to be construed and rendered, and the larger-size 3D virtual world scenes need to be transmitted back in DL. The decisions pertaining to computation offloading and channel assignment are optimized in the UL stage, and the MC will optimize power allocation for users assigned with a channel in the UL transmission stage. To ensure the reliability and low latency of the system, we design a novel Multi-agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (MADRL) structure, namely Asynchronous Actors Hybrid Critic (AAHC). The simulation experiments demonstrate that compared to the proposed baselines, AAHC obtains better solutions with a preferable training time.
Keywords: Reinforcement learning, resource allocation, latency, reliability, wireless communications, Metaverse
Research_Area: Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Digital Twin in Metaverse over 6G Wireless Communication
System_Architecture: The system architecture includes an asynchronous joint UL-DL scenario with two agents: UL Agent (Agent 1) for computation offloading and channel assignment, and DL Agent (Agent 2) for power allocation. It utilizes the Asynchronous Actors Hybrid Critic (AAHC) algorithm with a hybrid critic network.
Agent_Role: Agent 1 (UL Agent) optimizes computation offloading and channel assignment for XR users. Agent 2 (DL Agent) optimizes downlink power allocation for users assigned a channel in the UL stage.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism uses the AAHC algorithm, a novel Multi-agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (MADRL) structure. It employs two asynchronous actors (for UL and DL) and a hybrid critic to handle sequential decision-making, with stage-specific and global rewards.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments comparing AAHC with baselines (IteRL, CRL, Random Allocation) using metrics like global reward, total delay, re-transmission times, and uplink rate.
Experimental_Design: Simulations in scenarios with 3 channels and 4-7 XR users. Bandwidth set to 10GHz, data translation function simulated with random scaling, channel gains following Rayleigh distribution.
Challenge_Type: Ensuring reliability and low latency in asynchronous UL-DL transmissions for real-time digital twinning in Metaverse, addressing asymmetric data sizes and interference.
Research_Gap: Limited research on joint optimization of computation offloading, channel assignment, and power allocation in asynchronous UL-DL scenarios for Metaverse digital twinning.
Evaluation_Limitations: The model assumes fixed UL and DL times, and the data translation function is simulated with uniform sampling, which may not fully reflect real-world complexities.
Authors: Wenhan Yu, Terence Chua Jie, Jun Zhao
Source_Name: 2023 IEEE Conference on Computer Communications Workshops (INFOCOM WKSHPS)

Title: Multiagent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Avatar Migration in AIoT-Enabled Vehicular Metaverses With Trajectory Prediction
Abstract: This article proposes a dynamic avatar task migration framework for AIoT-enabled vehicular metaverses (AeVeM) to minimize latency and enhance user immersive experiences. The framework combines a coverage-aware LSTM trajectory prediction model with a Hybrid-MAPPO algorithm to address challenges like high mobility of vehicles, dynamic RSU workloads, and nonuniform RSU deployment.
Keywords: Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT), avatar, Metaverses, multiagent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL), service migration, trajectory prediction
System_Architecture: Decentralized Multiagent System
Agent_Role: Intelligent Vehicles, RSUs
Behavior_Mechanism: Hybrid continuous and discrete action spaces, POMDP
Evaluation_Method: Numerical simulations, MSE, R2 score, MAE, MedAE
Experimental_Design: Urban scenario with 10 vehicles and 6 RSUs, varying task sizes, bandwidths, and computing resources
Challenge_Type: Resource-intensive tasks, dynamic RSU workload, nonuniform RSU deployment
Research_Gap: Existing works did not address heterogeneous RSU deployment and the impact of vehicle mobility on avatar migration.
Evaluation_Limitations: Limited to simulation-based evaluation; real-world deployment challenges not addressed.
Authors: Junlong Chen, Jiawen Kang, Minrui Xu, Zehui Xiong, Dusit Niyato, Abbas Jamalipour, Shengli Xie

Title: Mobile Edge Adversarial Detection for Digital Twinning to the Metaverse: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
Abstract: Digital Twinning of physical world scenes onto the Metaverse is necessary for augmented reality (AR)-assisted driving. In AR-assisted driving, physical environment scenes are first captured by AR vehicles and are uploaded to the Metaverse for the construction of the Metaverse Map. However, the development of AR-assisted driving applications invites adversaries. These attackers may place adversarial patches on physical objects, seeking to contort the Metaverse Map. As real-time, accurate detection of adversarial patches is compute-intensive, these physical world scenes have to be offloaded to the Metaverse Map Base Station (MMBS) for computation. Therefore, we considered a scenario where AR vehicles capture physical world scenes and upload these scenes in real-time to the MMBSs. We formulated an optimization problem where the MMSP’s objective is to maximize adversarial patch detection mean Average Precision (mAP), while minimizing the computed AR scene uplink transmission latency and minimizing the worst-case (largest) AR vehicle’s uplink transmission battery charge consumption, through optimizing the AR vehicle-MMBS allocation, AR vehicle uplink scene resolution selection, and AR vehicle uplink power output selection. We proposed a Heterogeneous Action (HA) algorithm to tackle the proposed problem. Extensive experiments show our HA models outperforms baseline models when compared against key metrics.
Keywords: Metaverse, resource allocation, reinforcement learning, augmented reality, digital twin
Research_Area: Mobile edge adversarial detection for digital twinning to the Metaverse using deep reinforcement learning
System_Architecture: The system architecture includes AR vehicles capturing physical world scenes and uploading them to MMBSs. It features three RL agents: Agent1 for MMBS allocation, Agent2 for resolution selection, and Agent3 for power allocation, using the Heterogeneous Action (HA) algorithm.
Agent_Role: Agent1 handles discrete MMBS allocation, Agent2 selects continuous resolution values, and Agent3 chooses continuous uplink power outputs, all working to optimize mAP, latency, and battery consumption.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism uses the Heterogeneous Action Proximal Policy Optimization (HAPPO) algorithm, with three agents having distinct roles and reward functions, and a hybrid critic for personalized advantage calculations.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments comparing HA models (HAPPO, HAA2C) with baselines (IDA, CTDE) using metrics like mAP, uplink delay, and battery consumption.
Experimental_Design: Experiments with 4 MMBS and 7-10 AR vehicles, varying bandwidth, power, resolution, and mobility. Training for 1,000,000 steps with ADAM optimizer and random seeds.
Challenge_Type: Real-time detection of adversarial patches in AR-assisted driving, optimizing resource allocation for low latency and energy efficiency, and handling mobile edge computing inefficiencies.
Research_Gap: Limited research on joint optimization of MMBS allocation, resolution, and power for adversarial detection in dynamic Metaverse environments with mobile AR vehicles.
Evaluation_Limitations: The model assumes uniform mobility and fixed parameters, which may not fully reflect real-world AR vehicle behavior and environmental complexities.
Authors: Terence Jie Chua, Wenhan Yu, Jun Zhao

Title: Multiple-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Avatar Migration in Vehicular Metaverses
Abstract: This paper proposes a dynamic avatar task migration framework for vehicular Metaverses, leveraging blockchain-based Web 3.0 for secure data management. The framework uses a Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (MADRL) algorithm to optimize migration decisions, minimizing latency while ensuring continuous and immersive avatar services.
Keywords: vehicular Metaverse, avatar migration, multi-agent deep reinforcement learning
System_Architecture: Decentralized Multiagent System
Agent_Role: Intelligent Vehicles, RSUs
Behavior_Mechanism: Binary pre-migration decisions, POMDP
Evaluation_Method: Numerical simulations, reward comparison, latency analysis
Experimental_Design: Urban scenario with 12 vehicles and 3 RSUs, varying task sizes and bandwidths
Challenge_Type: Task pre-migration decisions, edge server overload
Research_Gap: Existing works did not integrate blockchain for secure data management or address dynamic edge server loads effectively.
Evaluation_Limitations: Limited to simulation-based evaluation; real-world scalability and security challenges not fully addressed.
Authors: Junlong Chen, Jiangtian Nie, Minrui Xu, Lingjuan Lyu, Zehui Xiong, Jiawen Kang, Yongju Tong, Wenchao Jiang
Source_Name: Companion Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2023 (WWW '23 Companion)

Title: On a Cooperative Deep Reinforcement Learning-Based Multi-Objective Routing Strategy for Diversified 6G Metaverse Services
Abstract: Metaverse has been widely recognized as an important 6G application with increasingly stringent, detailed, and diversified requirements for multiple Quality of Service (QoS) metrics. However, traditional routing strategies are usually based on unified weights and select the paths independently, which neglects the service diversity and resource constraints. In this paper, we focus on the diversified metaverse service requirements and propose the Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) based multiple objective routing strategy for different services. Multiple agents in the DRL model cooperatively select the paths to improve the resource utilization efficiency. Simulation results illustrate that our proposal outperforms traditional strategies.
Keywords: 6G metaverse services, diversified QoS requirements, multi-objective routing, cooperative deep reinforcement learning
Research_Area: Multi-objective routing strategy for diversified 6G metaverse services using cooperative deep reinforcement learning
System_Architecture: The system architecture is modeled as a wireless mesh network (WMN) represented by an undirected graph \(G(V, E)\), with edge servers near routers. It uses a Decentralized Partially Observable MDP (Dec-POMDP) framework.
Agent_Role: Agents are responsible for end-to-end (E2E) path selection for specific services, each making routing decisions based on neighbor router states.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism uses a Multi-Agent Deep Q-Network (MA-DQN) algorithm with a cooperation mechanism. It models the problem as a Dec-POMDP, where agents share rewards and cooperate to optimize paths for diversified services.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments comparing MA-DQN with DQN, Multi-Type Open Shortest Path First (MT-OSPF), and Routing Information Protocol (RIP) using metrics like rewards, average packet delay, packet drop rate, and network throughput.
Experimental_Design: Simulations use a network topology with 11 router nodes, 14 links, packet sending rate of 1.5 Gbps, and transmission rate of 5 Gbps. Weights for QoS metrics are set for latency-sensitive, high-reliability, and throughput-sensitive services.
Challenge_Type: Meeting diversified and stringent QoS requirements for multiple metrics (latency, packet drop rate, throughput) in 6G metaverse services and optimizing resource utilization.
Research_Gap: Traditional routing strategies neglect service diversity and resource constraints, and existing methods often use unified paths or single metrics, failing to address diversified QoS needs.
Evaluation_Limitations: The evaluation focuses on a specific network topology and may not fully account for all real-world 6G network complexities or dynamic service changes.
Authors: Bomin Mao, Xueming Zhou, Jiajia Liu, Nei Kato

Title: On a Hierarchical Content Caching and Asynchronous Updating Scheme for Non-Terrestrial Network-Assisted Connected Automated Vehicles
Abstract: This paper proposes a hierarchical content caching and asynchronous updating scheme for Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs) to optimize content delivery for Connected Automated Vehicles (CAVs). The scheme includes a Delay-Motivated Ant Colony Optimization (DM-ACO) algorithm for caching node selection and a Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning-based Hierarchical Caching and Asynchronous Updating (MADRL-HCAU) strategy to minimize transmission delay and improve cache hit ratio.
Keywords: Non-terrestrial networks, hierarchical caching, asynchronous updating, ant colony optimization, multi-agent deep reinforcement learning
System_Architecture: Hierarchical Multi-Layer System
Agent_Role: LEO Satellites, UAVs
Behavior_Mechanism: DM-ACO for node selection, MADRL-HCAU for caching decisions
Evaluation_Method: Numerical simulations, cache hit ratio, packet drop rate, content updating delay
Experimental_Design: Simulated urban scenario with 12 vehicles and 3 edge servers, varying content sizes and request frequencies
Challenge_Type: Heterogeneous caching hardware, dynamic network environments, content duplication
Research_Gap: Existing works lack integration of cross-layer caching and asynchronous updating for NTNs.
Evaluation_Limitations: Simulation-based; real-world deployment challenges not addressed.
Authors: Bomin Mao, Yangbo Liu, Hongzhi Guo, Yijie Xun, Jiadai Wang, Jiajia Liu, Nei Kato
Source_Name: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications

Title: QoE-Oriented Cooperative VR Rendering and Dynamic Resource Leasing in Metaverse
Abstract: The rise of the Metaverse has ushered in a new era of social networking, offering users deeply engaging spaces to connect and participate in social activities. However, rendering these virtual environments is resource-intensive. With many users accessing simultaneously and requiring diverse Metaverse services, optimizing Metaverse resources to deliver the best quality-of-experience (QoE) for users is a significant challenge. In this paper, we propose a cooperative virtual reality (VR) rendering and dynamic resource leasing mechanism to address this issue. Specifically, we first introduce a cooperative VR scene pre-rendering framework between users and Planets (i.e., edge servers hosting users), and establish a new user QoE metric named EdgeVRQoE which considers both rendering delay and visual quality. We formulate the multidimensional rendering resources (e.g., GPU, CPU, and outbound bandwidth) leasing problem between Planets and users as a double-layer decision problem, and devise a hybrid action multi-agent reinforcement learning-based dynamic resource auction mechanism to efficiently allocate limited resources of Planets in a distributed and adaptive manner. Extensive simulations demonstrate that our proposed scheme outperforms the representatives in user QoE and resource utilization efficiency. Particularly, the proposed scheme shows at least an 18-fold improvement in QoE over other schemes, demonstrating its capability in providing immersive Metaverse experiences.
Keywords: Metaverse, cooperative VR rendering, QoE, MARL, dynamic resource leasing
Research_Area: QoE-oriented cooperative VR rendering and dynamic resource leasing in Metaverse
System_Architecture: The system architecture is a three-layer cloud-edge-end collaborative Metaverse network, including a central Nebula server, edge servers (Planets), and user terminals. It uses a Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP) framework.
Agent_Role: Users act as bidders leasing rendering resources, and Planets act as auctioneers pricing and allocating resources. Users decide which Planet to access and resource quantities, while Planets set resource prices.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism uses the MA-HyAR-TD3 algorithm, a hybrid action multi-agent reinforcement learning approach. It constructs a latent representation space for discrete-continuous hybrid actions and uses twin-delayed deep deterministic policy gradient for training.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments comparing MA-HyAR-TD3 with baselines (MA-PA-DDPG, MA-PA-Delayed-DDPG, MA-PA-TD3, Random scheme) using metrics like average reward, EdgeVRQoE, pre-rendering success percentage, and resource utilization.
Experimental_Design: Experiments with 1 Nebula server, 3 Planets, 5 users; network distances [10,100]; 30 virtual object types; resource capacities varying. Trained with Adam optimizer, evaluated over 100 episodes.
Challenge_Type: Balancing visual quality and rendering delay, handling distributed discrete-continuous hybrid decision-making, and adapting to dynamic Metaverse environments.
Research_Gap: Existing methods lack efficient handling of discrete-continuous hybrid actions and comprehensive QoE models integrating delay and visual quality for multi-user Metaverse scenarios.
Evaluation_Limitations: The evaluation focuses on a specific number of users and Planets, and may not fully reflect extremely large-scale Metaverse environments or all service types.
Authors: Nan Liu, Tom H. Luan, Yuntao Wang, Yiliang Liu, Zhou Su

Title: QoI-Aware Mobile Crowdsensing for Metaverse by Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning
Abstract: Metaverse is expected to provide mobile users with emerging applications both in regular situations like intelligent transportation services and in emergencies like wireless search and disaster response. These applications are usually associated with stringent quality-of-information (QoI) requirements like throughput and age-of-information (AoI), which can be further guaranteed by using unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) as aerial base stations (BSs) to compensate the existing 5G infrastructures. In this paper, we consider a new QoI-aware mobile crowdsensing (MCS) campaign by UAVs which move around and collect data from mobile users wearing metaverse devices. Specifically, we propose 'MetaCS', a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) framework with improvements on a Transformer-based user mobility prediction module between regions and a relational graph learning mechanism to enable the selection of most informative partners to communicate for each UAV. Extensive results and trajectory visualizations on three real mobility datasets in NCSU, KAIST and Beijing show that MetaCS consistently outperforms six baselines in terms of overall QoI index, when varying different numbers of UAVs, throughput requirement, and AoI threshold.
Keywords: Mobile crowdsensing for metaverse, quality-of-information, user behavior modeling, multi-agent deep reinforcement learning
System_Architecture: Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP)
Agent_Role: UAVs as mobile aerial BSs
Behavior_Mechanism: Transformer-based user mobility prediction, relational graph learning
Evaluation_Method: Overall QoI index, throughput satisfactory ratio, AoI satisfactory ratio, episodic AoI
Experimental_Design: Three real-world mobility datasets (NCSU, KAIST, Beijing), hyperparameter tuning, ablation study, performance comparison with six baselines
Challenge_Type: Balancing UAV movements for data collection, saving battery power, maintaining QoI for all users
Research_Gap: Existing approaches seldom consider suitable collaborative control mechanism among UAVs and accurate prediction of the spatial-temporal user distribution
Evaluation_Limitations: Computational complexity increases with task duration and number of UAVs
Authors: Yuxiao Ye, Hao Wang, Chi Harold Liu, Zipeng Dai, Guozheng Li, Guoren Wang, Jian Tang
Source_Name: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications

Title: Resource Allocation and QoE Maximization in Aerial MEC-empowered Metaverse Service: A CCM-Multi-agent DRL approach
Abstract: The integration of Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) with aerial platforms introduces novel potential for the Metaverse world by providing low-latency and highly reliable computing and communication services at the network edge. Nevertheless, this integration presents critical challenges, such as low Quality of Experience (QoE) due to the dynamic nature of aerial platforms, high resource demands, and the requirements for real-time data processing in the Metaverse environment. To address these challenges, we propose a Combinatorial Client-Master Multiagent Deep Reinforcement Learning (CCM-MADRL) based joint resource allocation and QoE maximization framework to enable intelligent real-time decision-making in aerial MEC enabled Metaverse services. We form a collaborative ecosystem where agents are designed to represent both Metaverse service providers and aerial platforms to promote fairness and efficiency in resource allocation, as well as optimize service delivery. By incorporating CCM, our approach considers diverse metrics, such as latency, reliability, meta-distance, and energy efficiency, to ensure a holistic optimization of Metaverse services. The MADRL approach enables adaptive decision-making, allowing the system to respond to the dynamic and unpredictable nature of Metaverse applications. Results from simulations that mimic realistic Metaverse scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed CCM-MADRL framework in terms of improved service performance, reduced latency, cost, and virtual meta-distance, maximized average QoE utility of Metaverse users, and enhanced resource utilization compared to baseline algorithms.
Keywords: CCM-MADRL, meta-distance, Metaverse, Quality of experience, Resource allocation
Platform_Type: Aerial MEC and Terrestrial Network Platform
Research_Area: Aerial MEC-empowered Metaverse service architecture with Terrestrial and Non-Terrestrial Networks (TN-NTN), including aerial base stations (UCH, HAPs), Metaverse users (MUs), and Metaverse service providers (MSPs).
System_Architecture: Client agents represent MUs, deciding MSP selection and resource requests; master agents represent MSPs and aerial platforms, allocating resources and optimizing service delivery.
Agent_Role: Combinatorial Client-Master Multiagent Deep Reinforcement Learning (CCM-MADRL) framework, integrating combinatorial optimization with MADRL for adaptive resource allocation and QoE maximization.
Behavior_Mechanism: Simulation experiments comparing CCM-MADRL with baseline algorithms (MADDPG, DDPG) using metrics like QoE utility, meta-distance, latency, and resource utilization.
Evaluation_Method: Simulated TN-NTN environment with 90 MUs, 3 MSPs, 20 subchannels per ABS; parameters include computation capacity [20,200] GHZ, meta-data size [10,200] MB, and transmission power settings.
Experimental_Design: The experimental design simulates a TN-NTN environment with 90 MUs, 3 MSPs, and 20 subchannels per ABS, using 2000 training episodes, comparing against MADDPG and DDPG to evaluate QoE utility, meta-distance, and latency.
Challenge_Type: Dynamic resource allocation in aerial MEC due to platform mobility, high resource demands for Metaverse services, and real-time data processing requirements.
Research_Gap: Limited research on hierarchical, multi-domain resource allocation and MSP selection for TN-NTN integrated Metaverse services with dynamic environments.
Evaluation_Limitations: Simulations focus on specific scenarios with defined MU and MSP numbers, potentially not capturing extrame edge cases or large-scale deployments.
Authors: Abegaz Mohammed Seid, Hayla Nahom Abishu, Gordon Owusu Boateng, Aiman Erbad, Mounir Hamdi, Mohsen Guizani

Title: Reputation-Based Trust Assessment of Transacting Service Components
Abstract: We witness a paradigm shift where entities such as software applications, people, businesses and service providers increasingly interact in virtual rather than physical environments, such as social media, social commerce, and the metaverses. A key issue that emerges in such environments is how one entity can trust another. Here, the concept of trust is considered as a meta-requirement, that is, the level of belief a service requestor has that a service provider will provide the service in a way that meets the requestor's expectations. We refer to the service offering entities as service providers (SPs) and the service requesting entities as service clients (SCs). In this paper, we propose a technique that allows for evaluating trust and assigning reputation to various service providers by considering first their ability to fulfill their clients' expectations or policies, and second the reputation other service clients have, when acting as recommenders for the aforementioned service providers. In this work, service clients and service providers are considered as virtual entities that coordinate with each other, and may include physical users, avatars, micro-services, software agents, smart contracts or any other distributed inter-networked resource, without making any assumptions as to what a service client or a service provider entity is as long as it participates in an interaction.
Keywords: Distributed Components, Service-oriented Computing, Trust, Reputation Systems, Multi-agent Systems, Metaverse
System_Architecture: Decentralized Trust Network
Agent_Role: Service Clients (SCs) as recommenders, Service Providers (SPs) as service offerers
Behavior_Mechanism: Dempster-Shafer algorithm for ranking, dynamic reputation adjustment
Evaluation_Method: Stability in presence of malicious recommenders, speed of degrading service recognition
Experimental_Design: Simulated network of 1,000 recommenders and 100 service providers, random initialization, 200 iterations per experiment
Challenge_Type: Handling malicious recommenders, early detection of degrading services
Research_Gap: Existing systems lack dynamic assessment of both recommenders and service providers
Evaluation_Limitations: Limited to simulated environments, assumes initial stable state
Authors: Konstantinos Tsiounis, Kostas Kontogiannis
Source_Name: IEEE Conference on Computer Software and Applications (COMPSAC)

Title: Resource Allocation for Metaverse Experience Optimization: A Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Evolutionary Reinforcement Learning Approach
Abstract: In the Metaverse, real-time, concurrent services such as virtual classrooms and immersive gaming require local graphic rendering to maintain low latency. However, the limited processing power and battery capacity of user devices make it challenging to balance Quality of Experience (QoE) and terminal energy consumption. In this paper, we investigate a multi-objective optimization problem (MOP) regarding power control and rendering capacity allocation by formulating it as a multi-objective optimization problem. This problem aims to minimize energy consumption while maximizing Meta-Immersion (MI), a metric that integrates objective network performance with subjective user perception. To solve this problem, we propose a Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Evolutionary Reinforcement Learning with User-Object-Attention (M2ERL-UOA) algorithm. The algorithm employs a prediction-driven evolutionary learning mechanism for multi-agents, coupled with optimized rendering capacity decisions for virtual objects. The algorithm can yield a superior Pareto front that attains the Nash equilibrium. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm can generate Pareto fronts, effectively adapts to dynamic user preferences, and significantly reduces decision-making time compared to several benchmarks.
Keywords: Metaverse experience, multi-objective optimization, resource allocation, Meta-Immersion, energy consumption
System_Architecture: System architecture includes a base station (BS), Metaverse users (MUs), and virtual objects. It uses a wireless transmission model with Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications (URLLC) and a user-object-attention mechanism for rendering capacity allocation.
Agent_Role: Agents represent MUs, making decisions on uplink power, downlink power demand, and rendering capacity. They interact with the environment to optimize Meta-Immersion and energy consumption.
Behavior_Mechanism: M2ERL-UOA algorithm combines multi-objective multi-agent evolutionary reinforcement learning with a user-object-attention mechanism. It uses a prediction-driven evolutionary learning process and Nash equilibrium to generate Pareto-optimal solutions.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments comparing M2ERL-UOA with baseline algorithms (DCNSGA-III, PFG-MOEA, MOPSO, M2ERL) using metrics like Pareto front quality, system reward, decision time, and delay.
Experimental_Design: Simulated environment with a BS, MUs, and virtual objects. Parameters include downlink power (240 dBm), uplink power (30 dBm), rendering capacity (500 K), and dynamic interference noise power (3-6 dBm).
Challenge_Type: Balancing QoE and energy consumption in Metaverse services with limited device resources, adapting to dynamic user preferences and interference noise.
Research_Gap: Existing methods lack efficient handling of multi-objective optimization and dynamic resource allocation for Metaverse services with diverse user preferences and real-time requirements.
Evaluation_Limitations: Simulations focus on specific scenarios and may not fully represent all real-world Metaverse environments, especially with varying numbers of users and complex interference patterns.
Authors: Lei Feng, Xiaoyi Jiang, Yao Sun, Dusit Niyato, Yu Zhou, Shiyi Gu, Zhixiang Yang, Yang Yang, Fanqin Zhou

Title: Research on Intelligent Applications of Metaverse Battlefield for Human-Computer Interaction
Abstract: This study aims to utilize virtual reality technology to build a highly realistic metaverse battlefield based on Cesium for Unreal and Unreal Engine 5. By leveraging 3DGS rendering, Open-Sora 1.0 object-driven, and NLP multimodal interaction technologies, it achieves virtual reality multi-agent tactical confrontation decision-making deduction. The MADDPG algorithm demonstrates excellent performance in multi-agent confrontation tasks, with a win rate consistently above 95%. This research provides an efficient human-computer interaction interface (HCI) and immersive experience, holding significant application value for enhancing the tactical decision-making capabilities of military personnel.
Keywords: MADDPG Algorithm; Metaverse Battlefield; Unreal Engine 5; Virtual Reality; NLP Interaction
Research_Area: Human-Computer Interaction; Multi-Agent Systems; Virtual Simulation; Military Decision Support
System_Architecture: Modular system with Cesium timeline visualization, UE5 rendering pipeline, 3DGS rendering engine, Open-Sora 1.0 object-driven interaction, NLP multimodal input, and MADDPG-based agent reasoning
Agent_Role: Red and Blue team tactical agents; Human participant as interactive decision-maker
Behavior_Mechanism: Centralized training and decentralized execution with MADDPG; Goal-driven behavior trees; NLP command parsing
Evaluation_Method: Win rate tracking; Response latency; Comparative analysis with baseline methods
Experimental_Design: Multi-round red-vs-blue virtual scenarios; Human-in-the-loop intervention; Controlled VR simulations
Challenge_Type: High-fidelity modeling complexity; Latency in multimodal fusion; Realistic military scenario simulation
Research_Gap: Lack of real-world military personnel validation; Limited cross-platform generalization
Evaluation_Limitations: Limited real-world conditions; Small-scale user testing; Scene stochasticity constraints
Authors: Beijing KeCui Man-Machine-Environment System Engineering Technology Research Academy

Title: Scalable Consensus Over Finite Capacities in Multiagent IoT Ecosystems
Abstract: In the real world, consensus achievement is an adaptive and evolutionary process. Tailor-made mechanisms are engaging in the light of disputes to resolve emerging contradictions between the atoms. Most of the dominant blockchain architectures tend to oversee this fact. They come integrated with rigid, overkilling consensus-achievement procedures, which, in many cases, are not necessary or significantly beneficial. They often raise overwhelming demands on the nodes and prohibit the deployment of fully distributed and peer blockchain operation in the IoT world. Still, for the IoT ecosystems and the metaverse to become self-sustainable and self-evolving, robust validity mechanisms must be embedded in the atomic scale. This work investigates the process of building and sustaining scalable consensus policies over the trivial atomic capacities of the IoT ecosystems. For this, we deploy the IoT microblockchain framework as an atomic consistency tier and we define a primary set of validity rules that every node can easily carry out. The initial concept relies on Gödel’s incompleteness theorems and K. Friston’s free energy principle and is investigated under the perspectives of graph and game theory. To study the dynamic behavior of the system, a competitive game among the nodes is run. The Proof of Existence (PoE) is utilized as a universal proofing case. Its feasibility is demonstrated, and its complexity is analyzed under various system considerations. The findings prove that finite-capacity atoms can collectively and efficiently support verifiable validity and scalable consensus in the evolutionary IoT ecosystems and the metaverse, even under conditions of high diversity, trivial capacity, and eventual consistency.
Keywords: Artificial intelligence (AI)-intensive multiagent systems, evolutionary systems, IoT microblockchain, IoT sustainable transactional trust, peer IoT ecosystems, rational metaverse, reality modeling, scalable consensus, validity in IoT ecosystems
System_Architecture: System architecture includes a multiagent IoT ecosystem modeled as an evolving directed multigraph of transacting nodes. It uses the IoT microblockchain framework as an atomic consistency tier.
Agent_Role: Nodes act as autonomous entities (atoms) participating in transactions, with roles ranging from active nodes initiating transactions to passive nodes participating in transactions initiated by others.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism combines graph theory, game theory, and the IoT microblockchain framework. It uses the Fundamental Transitive Rule (FTR), the Seed of Truth, and the Fame Game to achieve scalable consensus.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments and graph-based analysis to evaluate the feasibility and complexity of Proof of Existence (PoE) under various system conditions, including finite memory and dynamic node behavior.
Experimental_Design: Simulated multiagent IoT ecosystem with nodes having finite memory capacity. The Fame Game is run to study dynamic behavior, and PoE is analyzed for different node birth policies and historical event horizons.
Challenge_Type: Achieving scalable consensus in IoT ecosystems with finite-capacity nodes, ensuring robustness against AI-fueled attacks, and adapting to dynamic user preferences and node diversity.
Research_Gap: Existing blockchain architectures overlook the need for lightweight, scalable consensus mechanisms suitable for finite-capacity IoT nodes, hindering fully distributed operation in IoT and metaverse environments.
Evaluation_Limitations: Simulations focus on specific scenarios and may not fully capture all real-world complexities, such as extremely dynamic or large-scale IoT ecosystems with varying node capabilities.
Authors: Aristidis G. Anagnostakis, Charilaos Naxakis, Nikolaos Giannakeas, Markos G. Tsipouras, Alexandros T. Tzallas, Euripidis Glavas

Title: Securing Distributed Computing in the Metaverse: A Balanced TGDH Encryption Scheme
Abstract: In this paper, we present a secure communication protocol based on a balanced Tree-based Group Diffie-Hellman (TGDH) key management solution for secure distributed computing in metaverse. The novelty of this work is that our modified TGDH algorithm balances the tree while preserving the distributed, secure architecture of group re-keying. The balanced tree ensures optimized computation regardless of join or leave events. This paper also presents a portable Python application programming interface (API) for the implementation of this secured group key management algorithm. This API addresses a lack of existing open-source implementations of TGDH, specifically in distributed-system architecture. From cloud based data sharing to distributed multi-agent frameworks and any secured computation application in metaverse, our algorithm can be easily and efficiently ported to any OS and used to secure group communications.
Keywords: secure group communication, group key agreement implementation, TGDH, secure multi-agent systems, security in distributed computing, secure computing in metaverse
System_Architecture: Decentralized Tree-based Group Diffie-Hellman (TGDH)
Agent_Role: Group members as equal contributors to key generation
Behavior_Mechanism: Balanced tree optimization, dynamic re-keying protocols
Evaluation_Method: Mathematical analysis, simulation of message length and computational efficiency
Experimental_Design: Simulated network with varying group sizes, comparison of balanced vs. unbalanced trees
Challenge_Type: Handling dynamic group membership, minimizing communication overhead
Research_Gap: Lack of open-source TGDH implementations for distributed systems
Evaluation_Limitations: Limited to simulated environments, assumes initial balanced tree state
Authors: Anway Bose, John L. Nori, Li Bai

Title: Smart Farm Security by Combining IoT Sensor Network and Virtualized Mycelium Network
Abstract: In today’s world, merging sensor-based security systems with contemporary principles has become crucial. As we witness the ever-growing number of interconnected devices in the Internet of Things (IoT), it is imperative to have robust and trustworthy security measures in place. In this paper, we examine the idea of virtualizing the communication infrastructure for smart farming in the context of IoT. Our approach utilizes a metaverse-based framework that mimics natural processes such as mycelium network growth communication with a security-concept-based artificial immune system (AIS) and transaction models of a multi-agent system (MAS). The mycelium, a bridge that transfers nutrients from one plant to another, is an underground network (IoT below ground) that can interconnect multiple plants. Our objective is to study and simulate the mycelium’s behavior, which serves as an underground IoT, and we anticipate that the simulation results, supported by diverse aspects, can be a reference for future IoT network development. A proof of concept is presented, demonstrating the capabilities of such a virtualized network for dedicated sensor communication and easy reconfiguration for various needs.
Keywords: smart farming, Internet-of-Things, mycelium, metaverse, multi-agent system, sensor network, artificial immune system
System_Architecture: The system architecture includes a metaverse-based framework with IoT sensor networks and virtualized mycelium networks. It uses OpenSimulator for simulation, with real-world sensors connected to a virtual environment through web services.
Agent_Role: Sensors act as sensing agents, user agents manage the system, and action agents execute responses. Transactor nodes facilitate data exchange between plant roots and mycelium.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism combines mycelium network growth simulation using the Monte Carlo method, a multi-agent system (MAS) for transactions, and an artificial immune system (AIS) for security and intrusion detection.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments using OpenSimulator to model mycelium growth, nutrient exchange, and alarm transmission between plants. Metrics include chemical activity levels and transactor data flow.
Experimental_Design: Six experiments with varying attacked plants, using 50 mycelium seeds, growth restrictions, and controlled nutrient and oxygen availability. Simulations run for 10 minutes, collecting data.
Challenge_Type: Ensuring robust security and efficient data distribution in smart farm IoT networks, mimicking natural mycelium behavior for scalable communication, and integrating AIS and MAS for adaptive threat response.
Research_Gap: Previous studies lack integration of AIS and MAS for IoT security in smart farming, and few consider virtualized networks for sensor communication and reconfiguration.
Evaluation_Limitations: The study relies solely on simulation without real-world implementation. Sensor placement and agricultural land limitations are not fully addressed.
Authors: Nurdiansyah Sirimorok, Rio Mukhtarom Paweroi, Andi Arniaty Arsyad, Mario Köppen

Title: Spatio-Temporal Multi-Metaverse Dynamic Streaming for Hybrid Quantum-Classical Systems
Abstract: This paper proposes an advanced multi-metaverse dynamic streaming algorithm in hybrid quantum-classical systems, consisting of three stages: QMARL-based spatial scheduling, temporal scheduling via modified bin-packing, and Lyapunov optimization-based dynamic streaming.
Keywords: Hybrid quantum-classical systems, quantum deep learning, metaverse, dynamic streaming
System_Architecture: Hybrid Quantum-Classical
Agent_Role: Observers, APs, Users
Behavior_Mechanism: QMARL, Bin-Packing, Lyapunov Optimization
Evaluation_Method: Simulation, Performance Metrics
Experimental_Design: Intel i9-10990K, NVIDIA Titan X GPUs, 128GB RAM
Challenge_Type: Limited qubits, High-dimensional action spaces
Research_Gap: First use of QMARL for metaverse spatial scheduling
Evaluation_Limitations: Limited to simulation, no real quantum hardware tests
Authors: Soohyun Park, Hankyul Baek, Joongheon Kim

Title: Semantic Information Marketing in the Metaverse: A Learning-Based Contract Theory Framework
Abstract: In this paper, we address the problem of designing incentive mechanisms by a virtual service provider (VSP) to hire sensing IoT devices to sell their sensing data to help creating and rendering the digital copy of the physical world in the Metaverse. Due to the limited bandwidth, we propose to use semantic extraction algorithms to reduce the delivered data by the sensing IoT devices. Nevertheless, mechanisms to hire sensing IoT devices to share their data with the VSP and then deliver the constructed digital twin to the Metaverse users are vulnerable to adverse selection problem. The adverse selection problem, which is caused by information asymmetry between the system entities, becomes harder to solve when the private information of the different entities are multi-dimensional. We propose a novel iterative contract design and use a new variant of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to solve the modelled multidimensional contract problem. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm, we conduct extensive simulations and measure several key performance metrics of the contract for the Metaverse. Our results show that our designed iterative contract is able to incentivize the participants to interact truthfully, which maximizes the profit of the VSP with minimal individual rationality (IR) and incentive compatibility (IC) violation rates. Furthermore, the proposed learning-based iterative contract framework has limited access to the private information of the participants, which is to the best of our knowledge, the first of its kind in addressing the problem of adverse selection in incentive mechanisms.
Keywords: Digital twin, semantic communication, contract theory, age of information, deep reinforcement learning
Research_Area: The research area is Semantic Information Marketing in Metaverse Ecosystems with Contract Theory and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning. It focuses on designing incentive mechanisms for virtual service providers (VSPs) to hire sensing IoT devices, addressing data transmission challenges and information asymmetry through a learning-based contract theory framework.
System_Architecture: The system architecture consists of a two-layer Metaverse ecosystem. The upstream layer involves sensing IoT devices collecting data and selling semantic information to the VSP. The downstream layer involves the VSP rendering digital twins and delivering them to Metaverse users.
Agent_Role: Sensing IoT devices act as data providers, the VSP acts as an intermediary for data purchase and digital twin delivery, and Metaverse users are consumers of digital twin services.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism combines contract theory with a new variant of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). It uses an iterative contract design to address multi-dimensional information asymmetry and incentivize truthful participation.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments using the CARRADA dataset, with metrics including VSP revenue, IR and IC violation rates, and convergence analysis. Comparison with a Naive MA-PDDQL baseline.
Experimental_Design: Training over 700 episodes with 200 iterations each, using 27 different sensing IoT device types. Evaluations under varying weighting factors, number of participants, contract items, and type distributions.
Challenge_Type: The challenge type is Information Asymmetry in Multi-Dimensional Contract Design for Metaverse Ecosystems. It involves addressing adverse selection problems arising from unknown private information (e.g., semantic extraction ability, transmission rate, refresh rate of IoT devices; valuation preferences of Metaverse users) and designing incentive mechanisms to ensure truth-telling while maximizing the virtual service provider's (VSP) profit with minimal Individual Rationality (IR) and Incentive Compatibility (IC) violations.
Research_Gap: Existing works lack solutions for multi-dimensional contract problems in Metaverse ecosystems, and few use MARL to address information asymmetry in incentive mechanisms.
Evaluation_Limitations: Simulations focus on specific scenarios and may not fully reflect real-world complexities, such as dynamic changes in network conditions and user preferences.
Authors: Ismail Lotfi, Dusit Niyato, Sumei Sun, Dong In Kim, Xuemin Shen
Source_Name: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications

Title: Securing the Metaverse: The Intersection of ML-Based Oracles and Blockchain Technology
Abstract: This paper proposes MARL-RSS, a framework integrating Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning and Ring Signature Schemes to enhance blockchain oracle security in the metaverse. It ensures data integrity, anonymity, and dynamic detection of malicious oracles.
Keywords: MARL, Blockchain, Metaverse, Machine learning, Oracle, Security
System_Architecture: Decentralized Oracle Network
Agent_Role: Oracles, MARL Agents
Behavior_Mechanism: Reinforcement Learning, Ring Signatures
Evaluation_Method: Simulation, Performance Metrics
Experimental_Design: Python-based framework, 5 agents, 1000 oracles, 1000 observations
Challenge_Type: Oracle tampering, Data integrity
Research_Gap: First integration of MARL and RSS for oracle security
Evaluation_Limitations: Reliance on simulation, no real-world deployment
Authors: Hajar Moudoud, Zakaria Abou El Houda, Bouziane Brik
Source_Name: International Conference on Intelligent Metaverse Technologies & Applications (IMETA)

Title: Tiny Multiagent DRL for Twins Migration in UAV Metaverses: A Multileader Multifollower Stackelberg Game Approach
Abstract: The synergy between unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and metaverses is giving rise to an emerging paradigm named UAV metaverses, which create a unified ecosystem that blends physical and virtual spaces, transforming drone interaction and virtual exploration. UAV twins (UTs), as the digital twins of UAVs that revolutionize UAV applications by making them more immersive, realistic, and informative, are deployed and updated on ground base stations, e.g., roadside units (RSUs), to offer metaverse services for UAV metaverse users (UMUs). Due to the dynamic mobility of UAVs and limited communication coverages of RSUs, it is essential to perform real-time UT migration to ensure seamless immersive experiences for UMUs. However, selecting appropriate RSUs and optimizing the required bandwidth is challenging for achieving reliable and efficient UT migration. To address the challenges, we propose a tiny machine learning-based Stackelberg game framework based on pruning techniques for efficient UT migration in UAV metaverses. Specifically, we formulate a multileader multifollower Stackelberg model considering a new immersion metric of UMUs in the utilities of UAVs. Then, we design a Tiny multiagent deep reinforcement learning (Tiny MADRL) algorithm to obtain the tiny networks representing the optimal game solution. Specifically, the actor-critic network leverages the pruning techniques to reduce the number of network parameters and achieve model size and computation reduction, allowing for efficient implementation of Tiny MADRL. Numerical results demonstrate that our proposed schemes have better performance than traditional schemes.
Keywords: Metaverses, multiagent deep reinforcement learning, pruning techniques, Stackelberg game, UAV twins (UTs)
Research_Area: UAV Metaverses with Digital Twin Migration and Multiagent Deep Reinforcement Learning
System_Architecture: The system architecture consists of UAVs, ground base stations (RSUs), and UAV metaverse users (UMUs). UTs are deployed on RSUs, and the system facilitates UT migration between RSUs to ensure seamless services for UMUs.
Agent_Role: RSUs act as leaders setting bandwidth prices, UAVs act as followers deciding bandwidth purchase for UT migration, and UMUs are service consumers.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism combines a multileader multifollower Stackelberg game model with the Tiny MADRL algorithm, using pruning techniques to reduce model complexity and achieve optimal resource allocation.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments comparing Tiny MADRL with traditional PPO, greedy, and random algorithms, measuring average reward convergence, strategy evolution, and impact of parameters like bandwidth cost and upper price.
Experimental_Design: Simulations with 5 RSUs, 15 UAVs, and parameters such as additive white Gaussian noise, channel gain, and transmit power. Experiments vary the number of RSUs/UAVs and analyze strategy convergence and reward changes.
Challenge_Type: Dynamic UT migration due to UAV mobility and limited RSU coverage, optimizing bandwidth allocation and pricing under information asymmetry, and ensuring immersive experiences for UMUs.
Research_Gap: Existing works lack efficient UT migration solutions combining Stackelberg games with tiny DRL and pruning techniques, and few consider metaverse immersion metrics in utility functions.
Evaluation_Limitations: Simulations assume unlimited RSU resources and fixed environments, not fully reflecting real-world dynamic UAV movements and changing network conditions.
Authors: Jiawen Kang, Yue Zhong, Minrui Xu, Jiangtian Nie, Jinbo Wen, Hongyang Du, Dongdong Ye, Xumin Huang, Dusit Niyato, Shengli Xie

Title: The impact of eye gaze on social interactions of females in virtual reality: The mediating role of the uncanniness of avatars and the moderating role of task type
Abstract: This study investigates how avatar eye gaze behavior (static vs. tracked) affects perceived quality of communication (QoC) and one-sided eye contact in VR, with a focus on the mediating role of avatar uncanniness and the moderating role of task type (communication vs. collaboration). Results show that uncanniness negatively impacts QoC but does not mediate the relationship between gaze behavior and social interaction metrics.
Keywords: Virtual reality, Eye tracking, Multi-agent, Avatar uncanniness, Communication, Collaboration
System_Architecture: Dyadic VR Interaction
Agent_Role: Avatars, Participants
Behavior_Mechanism: Static vs. Tracked Eye Gaze
Evaluation_Method: Quantitative Questionnaires, Eye Gaze Data
Experimental_Design: HTC VIVE Pro Eye, Unity, 44 Female Participants
Challenge_Type: Limited realism in avatar behavior, Gender bias
Research_Gap: Mediation effect of uncanniness not significant
Evaluation_Limitations: Female-only sample, Limited facial tracking
Authors: Aleksandra Zheleva, Julie Hardeman, Wouter Durnez, Charlotte Vanroelen, Jonas De Bruyne, Dennis Osei Tutu, Jessica Morton, Jamil Joundi, Jelle Saldien, Klaas Bombeke

Title: Toward Human Digital Twins for Cybersecurity Simulations on the Metaverse: Ontological and Network Science Approach
Abstract: Background: Cyber defense is reactive and slow. On average, the time-to-remedy is hundreds of times larger than the time-to-compromise. In response, Human Digital Twins (HDTs) offer the capability of running massive simulations across multiple domains on the Metaverse. Simulated results may predict adversaries' behaviors and tactics, leading to more proactive cyber defense strategies. However, current HDTs’ cognitive architectures are underdeveloped for such use. Objective: This paper aims to make a case for extending the current digital cognitive architectures as the first step toward more robust HDTs that are suitable for realistic Metaverse cybersecurity simulations. Methods: This study formally documented 108 psychology constructs and thousands of related paths based on 20 time-tested psychology theories, all of which were packaged as Cybonto—a novel ontology. Then, this study applied 20 network science centrality algorithms in ranking the Cybonto psychology constructs by their influences. Results: Out of 108 psychology constructs, the top 10 are Behavior, Arousal, Goals, Perception, Self-efficacy, Circumstances, Evaluating, Behavior-Controllability, Knowledge, and Intentional Modality. In this list, only Behaviors, Goals, Perception, Evaluating, and Knowledge are parts of existing digital cognitive architectures. Notably, some of the constructs are not explicitly implemented. Early usability tests demonstrate that Cybonto can also be useful for immediate uses such as manual analysis of hackers’ behaviors and automatic analysis of behavioral cybersecurity knowledge texts. Conclusions: The results call for specific extensions of current digital cognitive architectures such as explicitly implementing more refined structures of Long-term Memory and Perception, placing a stronger focus on noncognitive yet influential constructs such as Arousal, and creating new capabilities for simulating, reasoning about, and selecting circumstances.
Keywords: human behavior modeling, cognitive twins, human digital twins, cybersecurity, cognitive systems, digital twins, Metaverse, artificial intelligence
System_Architecture: The system architecture includes the Metaverse with interconnected Digital Twin (DT) networks, Human Digital Twins (HDTs), and nonhuman DTs representing various components like information systems. It features three environments: internal, in-group, and societal, following Bronfenbrenner's Ecological System Theory.
Agent_Role: HDTs represent human actors (malicious hackers, defenders, system users), while nonhuman DTs model technical components. HDTs interact with other DTs and environments to simulate cyber behaviors.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism uses the Cybonto ontology to model human psychological constructs and their relationships, combined with network science centrality algorithms to rank construct influences. It extends digital cognitive architectures to include noncognitive factors.
Evaluation_Method: Usability tests for manual analysis of hackers' behaviors using Cybonto, automatic analysis of behavioral cybersecurity research papers, and simulation of HDTs in different environments.
Experimental_Design: Workshop with cybersecurity professionals analyzing biographies of cyber actors using Cybonto, and natural language processing analysis of over 3000 behavioral cybersecurity research papers.
Challenge_Type: Developing robust HDTs for cybersecurity simulations, addressing underdeveloped cognitive architectures, and creating a reference ontology for behavioral cybersecurity knowledge.
Research_Gap: Current HDTs lack comprehensive cognitive architectures for cybersecurity simulations, and existing cybersecurity ontologies are fragmented with weak ontological commitments.
Evaluation_Limitations: Cybonto is in an alpha release stage, requiring cross-checking by psychologists and ontology testing. Implementations may face integrity issues from uncareful construct additions/removals.
Authors: Tam N Nguyen

Title: The Transformation of the New Media Communication Paradigm in the Metaverse Era and Blockchain Based on the Topological Characteristics of Information Communication
Abstract: Keeping pace with the times, this paper analyzes and interprets the concept, characteristics of Metaverse, and its influence on new media communication. Based on this, under the topological characteristics of information communication, this paper discusses the transformation of new media communication paradigm in the metaverse era and models the prediction of new media information dissemination in metaverse. In this paper, the multi-agent modeling method is used to analyze and compare the evolution results of each agent attribute under different parameter values. At the same time, based on the ideal social media network, the improved integrated immune control strategy is applied to the dynamic model of epidemic (SEIR) with latent period, and the spreading and evolution process of rumors before and after immunization is simulated. This research has both theoretical value and practical application value, which can provide a useful reference for the follow-up research of new media communication.
Keywords: Blockchain, Information Dissemination, Metaverse, New Media
System_Architecture: The system architecture integrates metaverse and blockchain technologies, combining multi-agent modeling and SEIR dynamic model to analyze information dissemination networks. It includes social media networks, government-media-netizen interaction systems, and uses topological characteristics like degree distribution and clustering coefficients.
Agent_Role: Agents include netizens as information disseminators/receivers, government as regulators, and online media as intermediaries, interacting within social network structures.
Behavior_Mechanism: The behavior mechanism uses multi-agent modeling to simulate agent interactions under different parameters and applies an improved SEIR model with immune control strategy to simulate rumor spreading, considering network topological features like degree, path length, and density.
Evaluation_Method: Simulation experiments on ideal social networks, BA scale-free network modeling, and prediction error analysis with 95.208% accuracy using Repast platform and dataset analysis.
Experimental_Design: Constructed BA scale-free network with 50 and 2000 nodes, used SIR model to classify nodes, simulated rumor spreading before/after immunization, and analyzed degree distribution and clustering coefficients.
Challenge_Type: Ensuring data privacy/security in metaverse, addressing computational resource demands, and balancing virtual-real integration in communication.
Research_Gap: Limited literature on metaverse-media integration, lack of comprehensive models for information dissemination dynamics, and need for more real-world data validation.
